title: Experiment-free exoskeleton assistance via learning in simulation
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"1bdnt","text":"Our experimental protocol details the human subject evaluation of a novel, simulation-based approach for optimizing exoskeleton control strategies. Utilizing multi-layer perceptron neural networks, this approach achieves: modeling human kinematics, optimizing muscle coordination, and automating exoskeleton assistance. This method capitalizes on the strengths of data-driven machine learning to refine control strategies, significantly reducing metabolic costs across various activities including walking, running, and stair climbing. This experiment protocol aims to collect metabolic rate and kinematic data during 3 activities (walking, running, stair climbing) in 3 conditions (No Exo, Assist On, Assist Off) to demonstrate the controller’s ability to generate smooth and synergistic assistance to different locomotion activities. The entire protocol can be completed in approximately 30 minutes for each activity of each subject (n=8). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dwm7l","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: To demonstrate the controller’s ability to adapt to different locomotion activities, we conducted a treadmill experiment for walking and running at three different speeds. The weights and biases in the controller network are taken directly from the simulation, and the inputs are thigh angles and angular velocities measured from one IMU sensor mounted on each thigh. These wearable sensor inputs are used to decode human intention and generate the desired assistive torque of the exoskeleton. The controller consists of a 3-layer neural network and is implemented on a desktop computer running Simulink Realtime. Our method does not require intermediate activity detection or gait cycle detection. The assistance torque is generated in real-time at each time step (100 Hz, namely, 0.01 seconds) using the current thigh angle and thigh angular velocity plus the history data from the past 0.03 seconds (corresponding to three time steps). Through training in the simulation, our controller learned to treat human movement as a continuous process and produces appropriate real-time assistance torque that is synergistic to the user movement during not only steady-state movements but also the transition phases. We conducted treadmill experiments (n=8) for walking at 0.75 m s -1 , 1.25 m s -1 , 1.75 m s -1 , and running at 2 m s -1 . We chose to test across gait speeds to demonstrate the generalizability of the control policy. The assistive torque generated by the controller learned in the simulation is adaptive to walking and running at different speeds.

To demonstrate the controller’s ability to generate smooth and synergistic assistance for three activities and their transitions, we conducted an activity-varying experiment in the real world for one participant. The participant started from slow walking at approximately 0.8 m s -1 , accelerated to running at approximately 2 m s -1 , then decelerated and finally began stair climbing (7 stairs) in a smooth manner.

For each activity: walking (n = 8), running (n = 8), and stair climbing (n = 8), we tested three different conditions, namely, Assist On, Assist Off, and No Exoskeleton. Two 9-axis IMU sensors mounted on both thighs were used to measure the joint angles and angular velocities.
Measure the metabolic rate during the Rest condition for 5 min.
Measure the metabolic rate, joint angle, joint angular velocity, encoder angle and velocity during the No Exo condition for 5 min.
Measure the metabolic rate, joint angle, joint angular velocity, encoder angle and velocity during the Assist On condition for 5 min.
Measure the metabolic rate, joint angle, joint angular velocity, encoder angle and velocity during the Assist Off condition for 5 min.
